segments.text: And now to our second candidate, Maria Fernanda Espinosa.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: She has served twice.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Women always want to do it twice.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: They do it so well that people ask them to do it again.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Twice as Ecuador's foreign minister, she was the Minister of Defence as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: In 2018, she was elected president of the 73rd session of the UN General Assembly.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: She's the first woman from Latin America to have that job, and only the fourth woman in the history of the entire assembly.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Maria Fernanda Espinosa is nominated by Antigua and Barbados.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Please join me in welcoming Maria Fernanda Espinosa.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Thank you very much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I love she's got good fashion sense.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Maria Fernanda Espinosa, you have five minutes to settle your vision.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Why do you want this toughest of jobs and what are you going to do with it?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: And I will give you a warning at four minutes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Thank you, Lise.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Excellencies and friends, it is a true pleasure to be back in London.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I was here already in January at the invitation of UN and UK and what I did is I spoke about a world in crisis.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Conflicts, famine, climate shocks, whining of global cooperation, the UN under strain.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I wish I could say things have improved.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And we know they haven't.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That is why I want to speak plainly this evening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Nobody wants another careful speech.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: What we need is we need action.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I want to focus on four things this evening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: First, I want to talk about prevention.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The UN was created to prevent war, a solemn promise made when bombs were still raining down in this city.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But too often, the UN arrives after the worst has happened.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: What we need is bold ideas.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: As Secretary General, I will establish a prevention and early action hub.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And its purpose is very simple.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: To ensure that warnings reach decision makers early and that action can be taken before the situation gets worse.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The Secretary-General must always be ready to lead these efforts.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: She must focus on addressing the consequences of a crisis, whether that's diplomacy to secure life-saving support or working together to manage energy shocks or food shortages.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And the Secretary-General must recognise when the UN needs to be centre stage, but also when it's a better place to play a supporting role.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Second, we must put people at the heart of development, not the UN.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: With support from countries like the UK, the UN has saved and transformed millions of lives.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We must not forget that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The Sustainable Development Goals remain the right ambition.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But ambition is not action.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Countries do not need another summit or another slogan.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: They need the money.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: the technology and the institutions to deliver for their people.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Developments cannot be delivered from New York.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I strongly believe that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We need a leaner UN that can focus on where it adds most value, a local first approach that empowers people and governments.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Third, we need to work together to harness and manage the power of AI.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: This is not a tech issue.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: It is a security issue.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: It is a jobs issue.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: It is a rights issue.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: No single state or company should call the shots.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The UN cannot and should not regulate everything, but it must bring people together, including civil society and young people, those that are normally shut out of these conversations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And finally, we have to change the UN for the better, not the cheaper.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yes, we have to cut costs.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That is essential.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But that cannot be our only goal.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Reform must mean doing better.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That means focusing on demand, not supply.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That means being prepared to let go of some functions entirely and focus on the organization's greatest strengths.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Ultimately, you, in this room and outside, will not judge the UN by the number of reform initiatives, nor by whether it has improved your lives and your future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That is the UN I want to build.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: With governments, with a strong leadership team, and with you, of course.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I do not pretend to have all the answers, but this is not a task for one individual.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The United Nations is a promise we must all keep.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So here's what I offer.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: A life devoted to serving my country and the international community.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: An insider-outsider perspective as someone who understands the system but is not captured by it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: A track record of working across all the pillars of the UN and with people from all walks of life.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: a firm commitment without fear or favor to the values of the Charter, to international law, and to serving all Member States and the world 8 billion people.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: This summer, I suspect many of you will be hoping that England's men's team will lift the World Cup after 60 long years of waiting.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Well, it has been 80 years of waiting for a woman to lead the UN.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So this is our opportunity to show the UN can walk the talk on equality and results.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: This is the United Nations I'm prepared to build.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Not a perfect institution, but a serious, compassionate one.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Not a louder UN, but a more relevant one that delivers where it matters most.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So you've talked about the wars, you've talked about the moments where the UN has to be centre stage, you've mentioned that no one power should call the shots.

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I think it's absolutely important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Okay.

segments.text: It can devastate and erase the entire infrastructure of a country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So it is the defining challenge of our time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We're running out of time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I have one last question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: And it is from Ian Bell from Northeast Hertfordshire of the Labour Party.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: in the United Kingdom, the governing Labour Party.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: His question, can we, given we are on course to fall short of the UN sustainability goals by 2030, do you think they have to be rethought to fit into today's ever increasing world of isolationism, tariffs and aid cuts?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: And if they are going to be rethought, how?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I think the Sustainable Development Goals are the right ambition.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I said that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: a reference and aspirational agenda.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I think it's very important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: It's one of the big successes of multilateralism.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And what I would do before we get to 2030 is put in motion the acceleration plans for implementation, of course, but I think that the UN cannot be
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: an implementer of development projects on the ground.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I said development has to be owned by countries because developing countries, and I come from one, we need three things.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And that's what I would work on.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The money, mobilized resources, the technologies, and the capacity, strong institutions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But the development cannot be delivered from New York.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: It has to be delivered under the rule of local first, the subsidiarity principle.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Last question but it's very short answer because we are over time but in interest of fairness, it's one of the toughest jobs in the world but the citizens don't want you falling asleep at your desk or going mad.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Very briefly, how will you relax?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Well, I will go to the first botanical garden next to me, if it's all buildings, because I think that nature gives you the energy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But I will also listen to elders and the elders that I grew up with.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: especially the ones I spent five years with in the Ecuadorian Amazon.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That was my school, my university, where I learned solidarity and resilience the most.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I will call one of them.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: They do have cell phones these days.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: At the time where I was living at the Amazon, they didn't.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So I will listen to the voices of wisdom and connect to nature.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: To some tree baby.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Maria Fernanda Espinosa, thank you very much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Thank you, Liz.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you so much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02
